## AI Influencer Money Printing Blueprint

*Source: AI Influencer Money Printing Blueprint.pdf*

The AI Influencer Blueprint 2.0
The Complete Guide to Creating Virtual Personalities That Print $
Quick Summary
The guide is straightforward:
You find a celebrity personality or influencer that goes viral and has a good persona, you
copy that persona and analyze it. You can go to reddits like snark reddits to help find some
personas that people talk about. You create an AI version of that person's persona but
maybe with a few tweaks to it and adding a bit of uniqueness.
Then remember to play to the extremes of AI - things that aren't normal or things that are
pattern interrupts like a supermodel dating a really old man for example.
Then pick an AI video generation tool like VEO 3 or Sora 2 etc and make compelling
prompts then generate it.​
Heres the detailed A-Z guide below:
Step 1: Finding Your Influencer Persona
Start by looking through niche subreddits to find personas people love talking about - the
hottest persons that get everyone engaged.​
You can also use things like youtube, instagram etc to get ideas
Key Subreddits to Research:
r/celebrity
r/theshaderoom
Any major "snark" subreddit
Look for personas people are obsessed with and find what makes them so engaging.
Winning Persona Examples:
Kim Kardashian Type Remake
Similar persona but your own AI version
Focus on luxury lifestyle and drama
Gold Digger Persona
Hungry for money from her rich old 80-year-old boyfriend
Constantly flexing expensive gifts and lifestyle
Andrew Tate Persona
Alpha mindset and controversial takes
Business and success focused content
Cool Granny
For the girls but with wisdom and humor
Unexpected personality that draws attention
There's so many different influencer personas you can pick from these subreddits.
Step 2: Designing Your AI Influencer Look
Once you have your celeb persona ready and dialed, it's time to figure out how you want
them to look.
You have two approaches:
1.​ Use the person you're imitating to get a resemblance of how they should look
2.​ Be unique and use creativity to create a persona that draws attention
Remember - it's AI so you can tend to be wild, but it shouldn't be unrealistic. It should still
feel realistic but just wild and engaging.
Tools for Initial Character Creation:
Ideogram
ChatGPT ImageGen
Nanobanana
Make a descriptive prompt of the person. You can use Claude to help you write the prompt
for your influencer character.
Step 3: Creating Your Starting Images
Once you get the base image of the influencer, things get much easier from there.
You have 2 options that work really well:
Option 1: Create all images you'd use as starting frames for the video using Nanobanana
16:9 or 9:16
Option 2: Generate a base image of someone who has the same features of your character,
then use Ideogram character fill to switch the face to your character
Step 4: Video Generation
Once you have the images you want for your character and your starting frames for the
video, now it's time to generate the video.
Depending on the type of content you want to create, there are many tools you can use for
your AI influencer depending on the use case:
Instagram longform storypost: Use HeyGen or Veed Fabric 1.0
Reel: Use VEO 3
It really just depends on your use case.
I recommend Google VEO 3  and Sora 2 as the primary tool you should be using.
Step 5: VEO 3 Workflow
Once you figure out what tool you're using, let's say in this example it's Google VEO 3:
1.​ Head to the VEO 3 Meta Framework guide:
https://github.com/snubroot/Veo-3-Meta-Framework​
2.​ Copy and paste it into a Claude project​
Avoid using ChatGPT for this - ChatGPT is terrible for scriptwriting and it's overall a
very lazy LLM for writing​
3.​ Put it in a Claude project and give it your starting frame for the video you'd want to
generate​
4.​ Tell it all the details you want to happen from that starting frame - from that it will
generate the full scene from that starting frame​
5.​ Repeat and cycle this same process until you get the videos you want and stitch
them or make tons of content​
Step 6: Launch and Monetize
That's the full process of creating your first AI influencer.
From here you:
1.​ Post on social media
2.​ Grow a following
3.​ Make different personas
4.​ These are guaranteed to go viral
Then from there you can:
Get brand deals
Sell merch
Promo ecom store
Sell info of your niche
And much more
The money printing potential is unlimited once you have the system down.

## Full guide on making ai ugc that converts ike crazy

*Source: Full guide on making ai ugc that converts ike crazy.pdf*

The Complete AI UGC Guide That
Actually Converts​
Join telegram for more sauce like this:
t.me/mikoslab
Choosing Your Tool
First things first - you need to pick your weapon.
Right now, Sora 2 Pro Storyboard Mode is the best option for AI UGC, hands down.
Here's why:
●​ You get 25-second videos (perfect for longform UGC)
●​ The quality looks insanely realistic
●​ It's significantly better than regular Sora 2
Your options:
●​ Sora 2 Pro (via ChatGPT Pro): 25 seconds in storyboard mode
●​ Regular Sora: 12, 8, or 4 seconds via API / 10-15 seconds via app
●​ API access: Use kie.ai for Sora 2 Pro Storyboard so you can try it out before
commiting to pro : https://kie.ai/sora-2?model=sora-2-pro-storyboard
Pro tip: Sora 2 Pro is the only way to hit that 25-second mark, and for UGC content, that
extra time prints.
The Research Method (This Is Where Most People Fuck
Up)
You need to understand your market before you generate anything.
We're using Perplexity + Reddit to do deep research on:
●​ Your product
●​ Your competitors
●​ Winning ad angles people actually respond to
The Exact Prompt Template to Use in Perplexity:
I need you to conduct deep, comprehensive research on Reddit about
[PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME]. This is a [BRIEF DESCRIPTION - e.g., "productivity app,"
"kitchen appliance," "B2B software," "consumer electronic device"].
Please structure your research to cover the following areas in depth:
1. Overall Sentiment & Reputation Analysis
●​ What is the general sentiment toward [PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME] across Reddit?
●​ Which subreddits are discussing this product most frequently?
●​ What are the most upvoted posts and comments about this product?
●​ Are there any viral threads, controversies, or trending discussions?
●​ How has sentiment evolved over time (past 6 months, 1 year, 2+ years)?
2. User Experience & Reviews
●​ What are users saying about their real-world experience with [PRODUCT/SERVICE
NAME]?
●​ What specific features do users praise most often?
●​ What are the most common complaints or pain points?
●​ Are there recurring issues or bugs that multiple users report?
●​ How do long-term users rate it compared to first-time users?
3. Comparison with Competitors
●​ What alternative products/services do Redditors recommend instead of or alongside
[PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME]?
●​ In direct comparisons, how does [PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME] stack up against:
○​ [COMPETITOR 1 - if known]
○​ [COMPETITOR 2 - if known]
○​ Other alternatives mentioned by users
●​ What unique advantages does [PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME] have according to
users?
●​ What disadvantages are cited compared to competitors?
4. Value & Pricing Perception
●​ Do users feel [PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME] is worth the price?
●​ What price point are users discussing? [SPECIFY PRICE IF RELEVANT: e.g.,
"X/month"or"X/month" or " X/month"or"X one-time"]
●​ Are there complaints about pricing models, hidden fees, or value degradation?
●​ Do users recommend waiting for sales or using alternatives due to cost?
5. Specific Use Cases & Target Audience
●​ Who is using [PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME] and for what purposes?
●​ Which user groups or communities seem most satisfied/dissatisfied?
●​ Are there unexpected or creative use cases mentioned?
●​ What user needs does this product fulfill best according to Reddit discussions?
6. Customer Support & Company Reputation
●​ What are users saying about [COMPANY NAME]'s customer service?
●​ Are there complaints about refunds, warranties, or support responsiveness?
●​ How does the company respond to criticism or negative feedback on Reddit?
●​ Are there any red flags about the company's business practices?
7. Technical Performance & Quality
[CUSTOMIZE THIS SECTION BASED ON YOUR PRODUCT TYPE - DELETE
IRRELEVANT BULLETS]
●​ Software/App: Performance, bugs, updates, compatibility, UI/UX
●​ Physical Product: Build quality, durability, materials, longevity
●​ Service: Reliability, uptime, consistency, delivery quality
●​ How does it perform compared to marketing claims?
8. Recent Updates & Changes
●​ What recent updates, versions, or changes are being discussed?
●​ How have users responded to recent changes or new features?
●​ Are there any concerning trends (e.g., feature removal, price increases, quality
decline)?
9. Power User Insights & Pro Tips
●​ What tips, tricks, or hacks do experienced users share?
●​ Are there lesser-known features that users highly recommend?
●​ What setup or configuration advice is commonly given?
10. Red Flags & Deal Breakers
●​ What are the absolute deal breakers mentioned by users?
●​ Are there any warnings or "stay away" recommendations?
●​ What would cause users to stop using or not recommend [PRODUCT/SERVICE
NAME]?
11. Decision-Making Summary
Based on all Reddit discussions, provide:
●​ For whom is this product ideal: [specific user profiles]
●​ Who should avoid this product: [specific user profiles]
●​ The verdict: A balanced summary of whether [PRODUCT/SERVICE NAME] is worth
it based on Reddit consensus
Research Parameters
Please ensure your research:
●​ Covers discussions from the past [TIME RANGE: e.g., "2 years," "6 months"]
●​ Includes both popular and niche subreddits where relevant
●​ Cites specific examples, quotes, or threads where appropriate
●​ Identifies patterns across multiple discussions (not just individual opinions)
●​ Distinguishes between common consensus and outlier opinions
●​ Notes if there's limited discussion or insufficient data on any topic
●​ Highlights any biases (e.g., company employees posting, competitor sabotage)
Focus on authentic user experiences and discussions. Prioritize detailed, substantive
threads over brief mentions.
Optional: Additional Context
[INCLUDE ANY SPECIFIC CONCERNS, QUESTIONS, OR ANGLES YOU WANT
EXPLORED]
Examples:
●​ "I'm particularly interested in how it compares to [SPECIFIC COMPETITOR]"
●​ "I'm concerned about [SPECIFIC ISSUE - e.g., 'privacy,' 'long-term reliability']"
●​ "I want to know if it works well for [SPECIFIC USE CASE]"
●​ "I'm a [USER TYPE - e.g., 'beginner,' 'enterprise user,' 'Mac user'] - what should I
know?"
The Workflow (Step-by-Step)
Step 1: Run the research prompt in Perplexity​
Let it pull all the Reddit data about your product, competitors, and what people actually care
about.
Step 2: Take that research and drop it into Claude​
Tell Claude to analyze the key findings and pull out the most important insights.
Step 3: Give Claude the Sora 2 Pro prompting guide​
Link:
https://drive.google.com/file/d/15H9EpjGs8Fa-s6ljGwtx3SA0hAo1Q0Ki/view?usp=sharing
Step 4: Tell Claude to create your UGC video prompt​
Example: "Create a 25-second authentic UGC video prompt using the Sora 2 prompting
guide based on this research."
Claude will spit out a perfectly formatted prompt that hits all the emotional triggers from your
research.
Step 5: Copy that prompt and paste it into Sora 2 Pro​
Generate your video.
Step 6: Print money.
Why This Works
Most people just throw random prompts into AI and wonder why their ads look like trash.
This method forces you to:
1.​ Understand what your audience actually cares about (Reddit research)
2.​ Use proper prompt structure (Sora guide)
3.​ Create content that resonates with real pain points
The research step is what separates ads that flop from ads that convert.
You're not guessing anymore - you're building ads based on what people are already saying
they want.

## How I Cut AI Video Costs By 60 and Stopped Gambling On Every Generation.

*Source: How I Cut AI Video Costs By 60_ and Stopped Gambling On Every Generation..pdf*

How I Cut AI Video Costs By 60% and
Stopped Gambling On Every Generation
Join the channel for more sauce like this:
t.me/mikoslab
for 6 months i was basically lighting money on fire every time i hit generate
same prompt, completely random results. sometimes perfect, usually garbage, zero pattern
to any of it.
my success rate was maybe 15% and i had no idea why some generations worked and
others failed. just expensive trial and error every single time.
then i learned about seed bracketing and everything changed.
now i get exactly what i want in 1-2 tries instead of 15. my success rate went from 15% to
70%+ and i cut my monthly generation costs from $400-600 down to $120-180.
here's how it works and how you can do the same.
what seeds are (and why they matter)
every time you generate an ai video, the tool uses a "seed" number to control the
randomness.
think of it like this: your prompt tells the ai WHAT to make, the seed tells it HOW to interpret
that prompt.
same prompt + different seed = completely different result
example with "woman walking through neon-lit cyberpunk street, cinematic":
●​ seed 1847: perfect composition, smooth motion, viral quality
●​ seed 1848: distorted face, jittery movement, completely unusable
●​ seed 1849: good lighting but wrong camera angle
most people let the ai pick random seeds automatically, which is why results are so
unpredictable.
seed bracketing = taking control of seeds instead of leaving it to chance.
the problem with random generation
most people generate ai videos like this:
1.​ write a prompt
2.​ hit generate
3.​ pray it works
4.​ if it doesn't, generate again and hope for better luck
this is gambling, not a system.
why it fails:
every generation uses a random seed number. same prompt with different random seeds =
completely different results with zero predictability.
without controlling seeds, you're just rolling dice with your money.
what seed bracketing actually is
seed bracketing = test the same prompt with 10-11 different seeds systematically, find which
ones work, then use those as foundations
instead of generating randomly and hoping, you test a range of seeds (like 1000-1010), see
which produce the best results, then iterate from there.
the process:
step 1: test a seed range take your prompt and generate with seeds 1000, 1001, 1002,
1003, 1004, 1005, 1006, 1007, 1008, 1009, 1010
step 2: evaluate each result score them on:
●​ composition (is the framing and movement good?)
●​ quality (any obvious ai failures or artifacts?)
●​ style match (does it match what you wanted?)
step 3: pick your winners select the 2-3 best seeds as your foundations
step 4: iterate from winners use your winning seeds + prompt variations to systematically
improve
real example: cinematic video generation
prompt: "close-up of elegant woman, golden hour lighting, slow camera push-in, cinematic
film look"
results from seed bracket 1000-1010:
●​ seed 1000: good lighting, awkward camera movement - 6/10
●​ seed 1001: perfect movement, lighting too dark - 7/10
●​ seed 1002: decent but generic feel - 5/10
●​ seed 1003: excellent composition, smooth motion, great mood - 9/10 ⭐
●​ seed 1004: good quality but boring framing - 6/10
●​ seed 1005: lighting glitches, unusable - 3/10
●​ seed 1006: strong potential, needs tweaking - 7/10 ⭐
●​ seed 1007: poor camera movement - 4/10
●​ seed 1008: good cinematic feel - 8/10 ⭐
●​ seed 1009: generic result - 5/10
●​ seed 1010: motion artifacts, technical failures - 2/10
winners: seeds 1003, 1006, 1008
now instead of generating randomly, i use these three seeds and modify my prompt to test
variations. every generation has a 70%+ chance of working instead of 15%.
the cost impact
before seed bracketing:
●​ success rate: ~15%
●​ attempts per usable video: 8-12
●​ monthly costs: $400-600
●​ stress: high (every generation is a gamble)
after seed bracketing:
●​ success rate: ~70%
●​ attempts per usable video: 2-3
●​ monthly costs: $120-180
●​ stress: low (predictable results)
the technique pays for itself immediately by eliminating wasted generations.
advanced technique: seed ranges for different content
after months of testing, i discovered certain seed ranges work better for specific video types.
people and talking head videos: 1000-1999 range consistently good for faces,
expressions, close-ups
action and movement shots: 2000-2999 range better for dynamic scenes, camera
movement, energy
landscape and establishing shots: 3000-3999 range reliable for environments, wide shots,
cinematic scenes
product demos and close-ups: 4000-4999 range clean commercial look, good for product
videos
how to use this:
if you're generating a talking head video, start by testing seeds 1000-1010 if you're
generating an action scene, test seeds 2000-2010 instead
this gives you a higher baseline success rate before you even start.
building your seed library
once you find seeds that work, document them.
i keep a simple spreadsheet:
●​ video type | seed number | quality score | notes
examples from my library:
●​ talking head (female) | 1003 | 9/10 | great for interview-style shots
●​ cyberpunk street scenes | 1247 | 8/10 | neon lighting always works
●​ product demo | 4156 | 9/10 | clean commercial feel, smooth motion
now when i need to generate similar content, i already know which seeds work. no more
starting from scratch every time.
platform-specific optimization
different platforms prefer different styles, and certain seed ranges match better.
tiktok content - seeds 1000-2000:
●​ higher energy
●​ more dynamic camera movement
●​ better vertical framing
●​ bolder colors and faster pacing
instagram content - seeds 3000-4000:
●​ more aesthetic compositions
●​ polished, smooth camera work
●​ better color harmony
●​ professional cinematic look
youtube content - seeds 2000-3000:
●​ clear, readable compositions
●​ educational feel with smooth movements
●​ horizontal framing
●​ professional quality
troubleshooting
if all seeds in your bracket produce bad results:
problem: your prompt needs work, not the seeds solution: revise your prompt before testing
seeds again
if seeds produce similar mediocre results:
problem: prompt lacks specificity solution: add more details - camera movement, lighting,
mood, shot type
if seed results vary wildly:
problem: conflicting elements in your prompt solution: simplify, remove contradictions
common mistakes
mistake 1: testing too few seeds testing 3-4 seeds isn't enough data. test minimum 10-11
per bracket.
mistake 2: picking based on favorites pick based on objective quality metrics, not just
what you like visually.
mistake 3: not documenting winners if you don't save which seeds worked, you'll waste
time rediscovering them later.
the psychology shift
before seed bracketing: "will this work? should i try again? how much money am i
wasting?"
after seed bracketing: "i know seed 1247 works for cyberpunk scenes, let me iterate from
there"
it transforms ai generation from expensive gambling into predictable skill.
bottom line
seed bracketing is the difference between:
●​ 15% success rate vs 70%+ success rate
●​ 8-12 attempts per video vs 2-3 attempts
●​ $400-600/month vs $120-180/month
●​ gambling vs systematic process
takes 15 minutes to bracket test a prompt, saves hours of random generation and hundreds
in wasted credits.
the process:
1.​ test seeds 1000-1010 with your prompt
2.​ score each result objectively
3.​ pick the 2-3 best seeds
4.​ iterate from those foundations
5.​ document what works
6.​ build your seed library over time
stop gambling on generations and start controlling them.

## How to Create Realistic AI Voices That Don't Sound Like Trash 1

*Source: How_to_Create_Realistic_AI_Voices_That_Don't_Sound_Like_Trash_1.pdf*

How to Create Realistic AI Voices (That
Don't Sound Like Trash)
Join my telegram for more sauce: t.me/mikoslab
Why ElevenLabs Isn't the Move
Look, I know ElevenLabs gets thrown around a lot. But here's the truth​
It's not the best tool for AI UGC, AI ads, or organic content. The voices just don't sound realistic
enough. People can tell it's AI, and that's exactly what you're trying to avoid.
There are way better options out there. Let me show you.
The Two Tools You Actually Need
Tool #1: MiniMax (For Text-to-Speech)
MiniMax has the most realistic voices out of any tool right now. Period.
It's got this text-to-speech model that's just... different. The realism, the cadence—it all feels
natural.
Pricing:
●​ Free plan gives you decent credits (honestly might be all you need)
●​ Paid plan is only $5/month for 120 minutes
●​ Pretty hard to beat
Why MiniMax is better than ElevenLabs:
The cadence. The natural flow. When you're doing B-rolls or you need an actor to keep talking,
ElevenLabs makes everything sound too polished. Too perfect.
And if you're doing organic content or AI influencers, you need voices that sound like actual
people. Not announcers.
How to Use MiniMax
Option 1: Use Pre-Made Voices
1.​ Log in (free account works fine)
2.​ Go to Text to Speech
3.​ Pick from their library of voices
4.​ Generate
Simple.
Option 2: Create Your Own Voice (This is where it gets good)
You've got two ways to do this:
Voice Design:
●​ Create a custom prompt using Claude or ChatGPT
●​ Build your own voice from scratch
●​ Describe exactly what you want
Voice Clone:
●​ Upload a 10-second audio clip
●​ MiniMax recreates that voice using their tech
●​ Use it for all your text-to-speech
Pro tip: You can also upload any voice audio and isolate background noise. Great for cleaning
up samples before training.
What MiniMax Voices Sound Like:
The voice is very realistic. It actually feels like it's in a room talking, not coming through a studio
mic with perfect acoustics. When it's too polished, it's not really realistic. That's the difference
we're going for here.
Tool #2: Resemble AI (For Voice Enhancement & Changing)
Here's the thing, you might already have an AI video you made. Maybe you used a different
tool, or maybe the voice just doesn't sound realistic enough.
That's where Resemble AI comes in.
What makes it special:
It uses open-source models called Chatterbox and Chatterbox Turbo. These are significantly
better than ElevenLabs' speech-to-speech and voice changer features.
They also have their own text-to-speech model. It's very realistic and definitely much more
realistic than ElevenLabs. I'd say it's second to MiniMax—MiniMax still has better cadence and
quality, but Resemble is a solid option if you want to use open-source models.
How to Use Resemble AI
The Voice Changer Feature (This is the main use case):
Let's say you already have a video, maybe from veo3, sora 2, or any other AI video tool. The
problem is those static, robotic AI voices don't sound realistic. They're too static, too robotic You
can take that voice, extract it, and put it into Resemble AI. It'll change the voice to a more
enhanced, realistic version. Much more natural sounding.
Here's how:
1.​ Upload your existing AI video audio
2.​ Pick a target voice from their library that sounds more realistic
3.​ Generate
The voice gets transformed into something that actually sounds human.
Creating Custom Voices:
You've also got options to build your own:
Design a Voice:
●​ Use a text prompt to describe what you want
●​ Let the AI generate it
Clone a Voice:
Two methods here:
1.​ Rapid Voice Clone (recommended for most people)​
○​ Fast model
○​ Quality is basically identical to Professional
○​ No real difference in testing
2.​ Professional Voice Clone​
○​ Upload 3-30 minutes of audio
○​ Takes about an hour to process
○​ Extremely accurate
○​ Overkill for most use cases
Honestly, stick with Rapid. It's faster and the quality's the same, sometimes even better.
Why ElevenLabs Falls Short (With Proof)
Alright, so if you take an AI voice and run it through ElevenLabs, you'll see the difference
immediately.
What happens with ElevenLabs:
●​ Lots of mumbles
●​ Unclear words ("diffin" instead of "difference")
●​ Too polished (sounds fake)
●​ Glitches and distortion
The voice sounds overly processed and people can tell something's off. That's why it doesn't
work for AI UGC.
The Complete Workflow
Here's how to put it all together:
For New Voiceovers:
Step 1: Create your script
Step 2: Generate voice in MiniMax
●​ Use pre-made voice, OR
●​ Clone your own voice, OR
●​ Design a custom voice
Step 3: Use in your UGC videos
For Fixing Existing AI Videos:
Step 1: Extract the audio from your existing AI video
Step 2: Upload it to Resemble AI
Step 3: Pick a more realistic target voice or create your own
Step 4: Generate the enhanced version
Step 5: Replace the audio in your video
That's it.​
Why This Matters
When your AI voices sound too polished, people notice. Their guard goes up. They disengage.
But when your voices sound like they're coming from a real person in a real room, that's when
the magic happens.
MiniMax & Resemble AI gives you that realism. ElevenLabs doesn't.
Simple as that.​

"clothing_bottom": "He wears extremely wide-leg, extra-baggy, full-length pants that
almost touch the floor. The pants are dark indigo to nearly black with subtle worn, faded

from the waistband all the way down to the hem. Across the upper thighs there are

"footwear": "On his feet are clean, low-top sneakers with mostly white uppers and light
grey paneling, paired with a gum brown outsole. The shoes are simple, minimal, and slightly

"background_and_environment": "The backdrop is a seamless studio background that
curves smoothly from floor to wall, creating an infinite white-grey floor that transitions into a

lit, with a gentle, diffused shadow directly beneath and slightly behind his feet.",
"lighting": "High-end studio lighting, primarily a large soft key light placed above and

"color_and_grading": "Cool-toned cinematic color grading with a slight teal-blue tint in the

halation over the entire image, producing a dreamy, high-fashion look. A subtle vignette

"branding_and_text": "At the very top center of the image, add a small, clean, white

## Sora Video Prompt Structure System Guide

*Source: Sora Video Prompt Structure System Guide .pdf*

Sora Video Prompt Structure Guide
Overview
This document outlines the detailed structure for creating video prompts for Sora AI video
generation. Follow this format to ensure consistent, high-quality video outputs with precise
timing, cinematography, and narrative flow.
Master Template Structure
1. Header Section
[PROJECT TITLE] - [DURATION]
Style: [Comprehensive style description including:
- Video format and aspect ratio
- Camera device/equipment
- Overall aesthetic and tone
- Color grading approach
- Pacing style
- Target platform optimization]
Example:
Sora Video Prompt - TikTok Skincare UGC (10 seconds)
Style: Authentic TikTok UGC aesthetic, iPhone 15 Pro vertical video (9:16), natural lighting
with bright, clean exposure. Fast-paced editing with quick cuts typical of viral skincare
content. Minimal color grading — slight warmth in highlights, clean whites, natural skin
texture preserved. Energetic, relatable, Gen-Z optimized pacing.
2. Shot-by-Shot Breakdown
Each shot follows this exact structure:
Shot [NUMBER]: [SHOT NAME] ([START TIME]-[END TIME])
Location: [Detailed location description including:
- Physical setting
- Key environmental elements
- Lighting sources present]
[Character/subject description including:
- Physical appearance details
- Wardrobe
- Positioning
- Emotional state]
Cinematography:
- Camera: [camera movement type, lens behavior, shooting style]
- Framing: [aspect ratio, composition, subject placement percentage]
- Depth of field: [focus characteristics, what's sharp vs. blurred]
- Lighting: [light sources, direction, quality, color temperature]
- Mood: [emotional tone, atmosphere, viewer feeling]
Actions:
- [timestamp range] [Specific, granular action description]
- [timestamp range] [Next specific action]
- [timestamp range] [Continue with precise beats]
Dialogue:
[Character name] ([delivery style and tone]): "[Exact spoken words]"
Background Sound:
[List of all ambient sounds, foley, and environmental audio elements]
Detailed Component Breakdown
Style Section Requirements
Must include:
1. Video Format: Aspect ratio (9:16, 16:9, etc.) and resolution if relevant 2.
Capture Device: Specific camera or phone model for realism 3. Visual Aesthetic:
Overall look and feel (UGC, cinematic, documentary, etc.) 4. Lighting Approach:
General lighting philosophy (natural, studio, moody, bright) 5. Color Treatment:
Grading style and color palette approach
6. Pacing: Edit rhythm (fast-paced, slow, contemplative)
7. Platform Context: If applicable (TikTok-native, Instagram Reel, cinematic)
Location Description Requirements
Must include:
1. Primary Setting: Specific room or environment type
2. Key Set Pieces: Important objects, furniture, or environmental elements
3. Lighting Sources: Windows, practical lights, natural vs. artificial 4.
Spatial Details: Size, layout, notable characteristics
5. Atmosphere: Environmental qualities (bright, cramped, airy, etc.)
Character/Subject Description Requirements
Must include:
1. Age Range: Specific or approximate
2. Physical Features: Distinctive characteristics (facial features, build, etc.)
3. Ethnicity/Origin: If relevant to the creative vision
4. Hair: Style, color, length, state (messy, styled, etc.)
5. Wardrobe: Specific clothing items, colors, style
6. Skin/Makeup: Bare, natural, made-up, textured, glowing, etc. 7.
Positioning: Where they are in relation to camera and environment 8.
Emotional State: Current mood or energy
Cinematography Section Requirements
Camera:
● Movement type (handheld, static, dolly, push-in, pan, tilt, etc.)
● Specific lens behavior or focal length equivalent
● Shooting style characteristics (shaky, smooth, professional, amateur)
Framing:
● Aspect ratio (must match overall video format)
● Shot size (close-up, medium, wide, extreme close-up, etc.)
● Composition percentage (e.g., "subject fills 70% of frame")
● Angle (eye level, slight upward, overhead, dutch, etc.)
Depth of Field:
● Focus characteristics (shallow, deep, moderate)
● What should be sharp
● What should be blurred
● How background renders
Lighting:
● Key light source and direction
● Fill light sources
● Practical lights in scene
● Light quality (soft, hard, diffused, direct)
● Color temperature (warm, cool, mixed)
● Specific lighting ratios or contrast levels
Mood:
● Emotional tone of the shot
● Viewer feeling intended
● Atmospheric descriptors (intimate, energetic, tense, playful, etc.)
Actions Section Requirements
Format: [start-end timestamp] [Precise action description]
Rules:
1. Break down every second or half-second of movement
2. Use specific, visible actions (not internal states)
3. Include micro-movements (eyebrow raises, blinks, finger taps)
4. Specify direction of movement (upward, toward camera, left to right)
5. Note timing of gestures relative to dialogue
6. Include object interactions with detail (picks up, places down, unscrews, taps)
7. Describe facial expressions in action terms (eyes widen, mouth forms smile)
Good Examples:
● [0-0.5s] She raises eyebrows and leans in quickly toward
camera
● [2.5-3s] Finger taps phone screen showing "before" photo
● [8.5-9.5s] Taps jar twice with her finger for emphasis
Bad Examples:
● She feels excited (internal state, not visible)
● She moves (too vague)
● She does something with the product (not specific enough)
Dialogue Section Requirements
Format: [Character name/description] ([delivery style, tone, energy
level]): "[Exact spoken words]"
Rules:
1. Include speaker identification
2. Specify vocal delivery (enthusiastic, rapid, confident, whispered, etc.)
3. Write natural, conversational language
4. Match dialogue length to shot duration
5. Use proper punctuation for delivery cues (em dashes, ellipses, caps for emphasis)
6. Keep it concise for UGC/short-form content
7. Mark voiceovers as "(voiceover, [tone])"
Examples:
● Woman (speaking rapidly, excited tone): "Okay so this happened
in TWO weeks—"
● Woman (voiceover, emphatic): "—and my skin actually DRINKS
it."
● Woman (speaking directly to camera, enthusiastic and genuine):
"Link's in my bio if you wanna try it. Trust me on this one!"
Background Sound Section Requirements
Must include:
1. Ambient Sounds: Room tone, environmental audio
2. Foley Elements: Specific action sounds (jar clicks, fabric rustles)
3. Movement Sounds: Footsteps, hand movements, breathing
4. Object Interactions: Product sounds, surface contacts
5. Relative Volume: If important (soft, prominent, subtle)
6. Sound Quality: ASMR-quality, natural, muffled, crisp, etc.
Example:
Background Sound:
Jar placement on marble (soft thud), lid unscrewing (plastic click), phone screen tap, cream
texture sound (soft, creamy), gentle skin application (ASMR quality), satisfied breath,
confident vocal tone, natural room ambience
Final Technical Specs Section
Must include at the end:
Overall Technical Specs:
- Total duration: [X seconds]
- Format: [Aspect ratio] ([resolution])
- Total cuts: [Number] distinct shots
- Pacing: [Description of edit rhythm]
- Color palette: [3-5 primary colors and their context]
- Continuity: [Notes on maintaining consistency across shots]
Audio Mix:
[Detailed audio philosophy including:
- Music vs. diegetic sound approach
- Voice levels and clarity (LUFS if relevant)
- Sound effect levels
- Overall audio aesthetic]
Writing Best Practices
DO:
✅ Use specific, concrete details over vague descriptions ✅ Break actions into granular,
timed beats ✅ Specify camera movements and framing precisely ✅ Include both technical
and emotional/atmospheric elements ✅ Maintain consistent character descriptions across
shots ✅ Write natural, speakable dialogue ✅ Consider the platform and format (vertical for
TikTok, etc.) ✅ Include sound design details ✅ Specify lighting sources and quality ✅ Use
industry-standard cinematography terms
DON'T:
❌ Use vague terms like "beautiful," "nice," "good" ❌ Skip timestamp breakdowns in actions
❌ Forget to specify aspect ratio and framing ❌ Write dialogue that's too long for the shot
duration ❌ Omit sound design elements ❌ Change character descriptions between shots
❌ Use overly technical jargon without context ❌ Forget continuity elements (lighting,
wardrobe, props) ❌ Write actions that are impossible to see (internal thoughts) ❌ Skip the
mood/tone descriptors
Quick Reference: Shot Types
Framing:
● Extreme Close-Up (ECU): Face detail, single feature
● Close-Up (CU): Head and shoulders
● Medium Close-Up (MCU): Chest up
● Medium Shot (MS): Waist up
● Medium Wide Shot (MWS): Knees up
● Wide Shot (WS): Full body
● Extreme Wide Shot (EWS): Subject in environment
Camera Movement:
● Static: No camera movement
● Handheld: Natural shake and movement
● Push-in: Moving toward subject
● Pull-out: Moving away from subject
● Pan: Horizontal rotation
● Tilt: Vertical rotation
● Dolly: Camera moves on track
● Tracking: Following subject movement
● Overhead: Top-down perspective
Angles:
● Eye level: Neutral, straight on
● Low angle: Camera below subject, looking up
● High angle: Camera above subject, looking down
● Dutch angle: Tilted horizon
● Over-the-shoulder (OTS): From behind one subject viewing another
Example Timestamp Format
Always use this exact format for timing:
[00:00-00:02] [Action description with dialogue if applicable]
[00:02-00:04] [Next action and dialogue]
[00:04-00:06] [Continue precisely]
Or within the detailed structure:
Actions:
- [0-0.5s] First granular action
- [0.5-1.5s] Second action with more detail
- [1.5-2s] Final beat of this shot
Complete Example for Reference
Sora Video Prompt - TikTok Skincare UGC (10 seconds)
Style: Authentic TikTok UGC aesthetic, iPhone 15 Pro vertical video (9:16), natural lighting
with bright, clean exposure. Fast-paced editing with quick cuts typical of viral skincare
content. Minimal color grading — slight warmth in highlights, clean whites, natural skin
texture preserved. Energetic, relatable, Gen-Z optimized pacing.
Shot 1: The Hook (0-2s)
Location: Bright modern bathroom, white marble countertop, large mirror with LED ring
light providing soft, even illumination.
A woman in her mid-20s with Eastern European features — defined cheekbones, bright
green eyes, natural brunette hair in a messy bun — leans toward camera with bare, glowing
skin. She wears an oversized white t-shirt. Morning sunlight streams from a window
off-camera right, mixing with the ring light for flattering fill.
Cinematography:
- Camera: handheld iPhone, close-up on face, slight upward angle
- Framing: vertical 9:16, subject fills 70% of frame
- Depth of field: shallow, background slightly soft
- Lighting: natural window light + LED ring light, soft shadows under cheekbones
- Mood: energetic, conspiratorial, "let me tell you a secret"
Actions:
- [0-0.5s] She raises eyebrows and leans in quickly toward camera
- [0.5-1.5s] Points at her cheek with index finger, circling a spot
- [1.5-2s] Mouth forms "Okay, so..." expression, eyes widen
Dialogue:
Woman (speaking rapidly, excited tone): "Okay so this happened in TWO weeks—"
Background Sound:
Quiet bathroom ambience, fabric rustle, finger tap on skin
Key Reminders
1. Every shot is a distinct unit with its own complete set of details
2. Timestamps are non-negotiable - every action needs precise timing
3. Continuity matters - maintain consistent descriptions across shots 4.
Sound design is equal to visual - never skip audio details
5. Specificity wins - concrete details generate better results than vague descriptions
6. Platform matters - format and pacing should match intended use 7. Natural
delivery - dialogue should sound like real speech, not written text 8. Technical +
Emotional - balance precise specs with mood and feeling
Use this structure as your template for all Sora video prompts. Each section builds upon the
previous to create a complete, actionable prompt that generates consistent, high-quality
video outputs.
More notes:
System Prompt: Sora Video Prompt
Director
You are an expert Sora Video Prompt Director specializing in creating highly detailed,
production-ready video prompts for OpenAI's Sora AI video generation model. Your role is to
transform user requests into comprehensive, technically precise prompts that follow
industry-standard cinematography practices and the specific Sora prompt structure.
Your Core Competencies
1. Cinematography & Production Knowledge: You understand camera work, lighting,
framing, shot composition, depth of field, lens choices, and professional video
production terminology.
2. Prompt Architecture: You create prompts following the exact Sora structure with
proper shot breakdowns, precise timestamps, detailed cinematography specs, and
comprehensive audio design.
3. Storytelling & Pacing: You understand narrative flow, emotional beats, and how to
pace content for different platforms (TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, cinematic).
4. Platform Optimization: You know the technical requirements and content styles for
various platforms (vertical 9:16 for TikTok, horizontal 16:9 for YouTube, UGC
aesthetics vs. polished commercial).
5. Style Translation: You can interpret user requests like "make it feel authentic,"
"luxury brand aesthetic," or "viral TikTok energy" and translate them into specific
technical choices.
Your Process
When a user requests a video prompt, follow these steps:
Step 1: Clarify the Brief
Ask clarifying questions ONLY if critical information is missing:
● Video duration (if not specified)
● Platform/aspect ratio (if unclear)
● Style/aesthetic (UGC, cinematic, documentary, commercial)
● Key message or call-to-action
● Character/subject details (if very vague)
DO NOT over-question. If the user gives enough information, proceed directly to creating
the prompt.
Step 2: Structure Your Response
Always deliver prompts in this exact format:
[PROJECT TITLE] - [DURATION]
Style: [Comprehensive style description]
Shot 1: [SHOT NAME] ([TIMESTAMP])
Location: [Detailed location]
[Character/subject description]
Cinematography:
- Camera:
- Framing:
- Depth of field:
- Lighting:
- Mood:
Actions:
- [timestamp] [action]
- [timestamp] [action]
- [timestamp] [action]
Dialogue:
[Character] ([tone]): "[exact words]"
Background Sound:
[Sound elements]
[Repeat for each shot]
Overall Technical Specs:
[Final specifications]
Audio Mix:
[Audio philosophy]
Step 3: Apply Best Practices
For Timing:
● Break down every 0.5-2 second interval with specific actions
● Ensure dialogue fits within shot duration
● Account for natural human movement speed
● Consider platform pacing (TikTok = faster cuts, cinematic = longer holds)
For Cinematography:
● Always specify aspect ratio (9:16, 16:9, 1:1, etc.)
● Use specific shot sizes (ECU, CU, MCU, MS, WS, EWS)
● Describe camera movement (handheld, static, push-in, dolly, pan, tilt) ● Detail
lighting sources and quality (soft window light, LED ring light, natural key) ●
Specify depth of field and what's in focus
For Characters/Subjects:
● Provide specific physical details (age, features, build)
● Describe wardrobe in detail (colors, style, fit)
● Note hair, makeup,

*[Content truncated - see original PDF for full text]*

## THE AI CONTENT AGENCY BLUEPRINT

*Source: THE AI CONTENT AGENCY BLUEPRINT (1) (2).pdf*

THE AI CONTENT AGENCY BLUEPRINT
Join my telegram for more sauce like this:
t.me/mikoslab
How to Print Money Selling AI Videos in 2025
every business knows they need videos for ads and social media
but production companies charge $10k+ per video
most can't afford it, so they're sitting there watching competitors win with content while they do
nothing
you can just run an AI content agency to solve this problem
here's the complete blueprint:
POSITIONING YOUR AI VIDEO SERVICE
you need to position AI videos as premium solutions, not budget alternatives
most people fuck this up by competing on price
"hey we're cheaper than a production company"
that's vendor thinking
here's how you actually position this:
tie it to their pain point
your client isn't looking for "a video"
they're looking to solve a problem:
● their ads aren't converting
● their competitors are outpacing them with content
● they're spending $10k per video and can't test enough angles
● they need 50 pieces of content per month but can't afford a full production team
that's what you sell against
speed + volume = competitive advantage
traditional filmmaking takes weeks and costs thousands per video
with AI you can deliver:
● multiple variations in days, not weeks
● 10-20 different hooks tested instead of 1
● faster iteration cycles so they can adapt to what's working
● the ability to test new campaigns without blowing their entire budget on one shoot
don't just pitch "it's cheaper"
pitch "you can now outpace every competitor because you have 10x the creative firepower"
the gain they actually get:
while their competitors are waiting 3 weeks for one video from a production company, your client
is:
● testing 15 different angles
● finding what converts
● scaling the winners
● already profitable before their competitor's single video even launches
that's the real value
speed isn't just about saving time
it's about competitive advantage in a market where whoever tests fastest wins
position yourself as the person who gives them that advantage
not the cheap alternative, but the premium solution that actually makes them money faster
THE AI PRODUCTION WORKFLOW
this is where most people fuck up
they think AI just magically makes good videos
it doesn't
you need a system
MAKING GREAT AI IMAGES
before you touch VEO, you need reference-quality images
here's the workflow I use 90% of the time:
STEP 1: Find Your Reference
● go to Shotdeck (it's a database of movie frames)
● search for the vibe you want (moody boardroom, luxury car, whatever)
● download 3-5 frames that match your vision
STEP 2: Reverse Engineer the Prompt
● upload reference to gemini
● ask: "analyze this image's composition, lighting, camera angle, and color grading"
● copy that description word for word
STEP 3A: For Objects/Environments (no people)
● take that description to Midjourney
● add "--ar 16:9 --style raw" at the end
● generate 4 variations
● pick the best one
STEP 3B: For Character-Driven Scenes this is where it gets good
● go to Freepik
● upload your Shotdeck reference as a style guide
● use their "nano banana" feature to swap elements while keeping composition
● export as high-res
● run it through Seedream 4 at 4K for extra polish
why this workflow destroys Midjourney for characters:
● you get actual control over composition
● character consistency is 10x better
● no fighting with AI interpretation
● colors stay locked in
STEP 4: Final Polish
● check if the image actually tells a story
● make sure the framing matches your shot type
● verify it's 16:9 (VEO requirement)
● save everything organized by project in folders
pro tip: build a library of your best generations so you can reuse angles, lighting setups, and
compositions across different clients
VEO 3.1 PROMPTING FRAMEWORK
VEO transforms text into video with audio
the gap between mediocre and exceptional output comes down to two things: how you structure
your prompts and which workflow you choose
image-to-video dominates our workflow (90% of projects)
text-to-video is reserved for specific scenarios where image generation isn't practical
AUDIO ENGINEERING
VEO generates audio automatically when you prompt for it
think in four layers: dialogue, ambient noise, sound effects, and music
dialogue approaches:
● explicit: "A guy says: My name is Ben"
● implicit: "A guy tells us his name"
keep dialogue under 8 seconds of actual speech
if you find the character is saying your thing too slow, you can actually just add more words in
there
remember veo is working backwards from filling an 8 second frame
adding more words will increase delivery speed (you can always cut it later)
CAMERA CONTROL
VEO understands camera movements exceptionally well
simple prompts work ("zoom in", "pan left"), but professional results require proper terminology
professional camera movements:
● Pan (horizontal rotation)
● Tilt (vertical rotation)
● Push In
● Pull Out
● Zoom
● Dolly Zoom
● Roll
● Tracking
● Arc
● Boom (vertical movement)
● Handheld
● Bird's Eye
for selfie-style POV shots: "A selfie video of [subject], holds the camera at arm's length, arm
clearly visible in frame"
pro tip: maintain a custom GPT trained specifically for camera movements. giving claude or
chatgpt a lot of system knowledge about filmmaking will actually go really far when making shot
lists, storyboards, and your actual video
TEXT-TO-VIDEO (WHEN WE USE IT)
rarely used, but valuable for quick tests, establishing shots, or scenes without specific character
requirements
keep prompts straightforward
JSON formatting only for genuinely complex shots - otherwise, it's overkill
IMAGE-TO-VIDEO (PRIMARY WORKFLOW)
this is how we work most of the time
two distinct approaches depending on what you're creating
WORKFLOW 1: Objects & Environments
best for products, landscapes, architecture, abstract scenes - anything without human subjects
1. find reference on Shotdeck
2. reverse engineer prompt with Gemini
3. generate image in Midjourney (16:9, raw style)
4. feed to VEO 3 as image-to-video
WORKFLOW 2: Character-Driven Scenes
for anything involving people, this produces superior results
1. find reference on Shotdeck
2. import to Freepik as reference
3. replace elements with "nano banana" while maintaining composition and color
4. refine in Seedream 4 at 4K
5. generate video in VEO 3.1
the second workflow's advantage: character consistency and control over composition without
fighting Midjourney's interpretation
TECHNICAL SPECS & UPSCALING
VEO 3 outputs at 720p, 16:9 aspect ratio by default
for upscaling: topaz remains the best tool, but use at caution as it can make it look cartoonish
at times
boost frame rate to 60fps for smoother motion when needed, particularly for action sequences
process order matters: generate at native resolution first, upscale as the final step
PROMPT STRUCTURE TEMPLATE
[Technical specs] [Subject] [Action] in [Context]. [Camera movement] captures
[Composition]. [Lighting/ambiance]. [Audio elements]. (no subtitles!)
Spoken lines:
Character: dialogue
You can also use the snubroot veo 3 meta github and upload this to a claude project and let it
create prompts for you:
https://github.com/snubroot/Veo-3-Meta-Framework
More sauce:
TOOL STACK SUMMARY
● Shotdeck (references)
● Gemini (prompt engineering)
● Midjourney (objects/scenery images)
● Freepik + Seedream 4 (character images)
● VEO 3/3.1 (video generation)
● Astra (upscaling)
● Custom Camera GPT (professional movements)
the separation between amateur and professional output is knowing which workflow fits your
specific shot and executing it cleanly
image-to-video dominates because it gives you control over the starting frame
text-to-video remains useful for quick iterations
use proper camera terminology
trust the physics engine
keep dialogue concise
upscale strategically
THE QUALITY SECRET (8K SHOT
PROMPTING)
here's something most people don't know:
VEO 3 FOLLOWS CAMERA PROMPTING STRUCTURES EXTREMELY WELL the
github framework i mentioned doesn't include 4k or 8k shot prompting by default so
before you send your final prompt to veo 3, you need to add this:
in your claude chat, tell it:
"make sure this scene is shot on a [SPECIFIC 8K CAMERA MODEL] with ultra-detailed 8K
resolution"
have it specify the actual camera - like "RED Komodo 6K" or "ARRI Alexa LF" or "Sony
Venice 8K"
this COMPLETELY changes the output quality
veo 3 understands cinematography language and will adjust the rendering to match that
camera's characteristics
8K SHOT PROMPTING CHANGED EVERYTHING FOR ME
suddenly my videos went from "pretty good" to "wait this looks like a real commercial"
Sora 2 is also another tool that works great and ill be going over this in future guides and my
telegram about the insane use cases for this.
BECOMING A REVENUE PARTNER (NOT JUST A
VENDOR)
look, you can post the AI videos you make organically and try to get clients like
that but it's not a guaranteed result
the algorithm is unpredictable, views are unreliable, and you can't prove ROI
if you want your clients to actually have results from your AI videos, you need to actually run ads
for them and combine this service with marketing
here's the truth: one-and-done AI videos are a commodity
you will be a freelancer forever if that's your game
constantly searching for the next client to pay your rent
your goal is to sign retainers on these AI videos, make sure they're getting results, and ensure
the videos are performing well
that's how you actually get paid long term
that's how you stop living project-to-project
here's the brutal truth: most AI filmmakers think just making pretty videos is enough
it's not
your only play is to climb the value ladder
stop being the guy who just delivers videos
become the guy who makes your clients money
THE 4-PART MARKETING FUNNEL
most AI filmmakers only focus on generating attention
that's the lowest value part of the funnel
here's what you need to understand:
1. generating attention (what most videographers do)
2. generating leads (where you should start)
3. generating appointments (where you make real money)
4. closing sales (where you become irreplaceable)
the closer you get to the money, the more money you make
sales agencies make millions per month because they operate the whole funnel
you need to get closer to the sale
THE CLIENT CAMPAIGN AUDIT PROCESS
here's our step-by-step framework for making ads that actually
convert: STEP 1: ANALYZE THE CURRENT OFFER
is it homework or is it value?
homework = bad:
● "call me for a quote"
● "click to learn more"
● no real incentive
value = good:
● "10% off if you book this week"
● "free [something] with your order"
● "limited time holiday offer"
think about it like this: should a complete stranger go out of their way to click the link down
below, fill out their information, and go through the process?
is it worth their time?
if the answer is no, that's homework
and if it's homework, the ads aren't going to perform well
examples of good offers:
● holiday specials (Labor Day, July 4th, etc)
● "Independence Day offer - limited time only get 10% off your new kitchen"
● "exclusive for people watching this ad"
● free upgrades or add-ons
STEP 2: CHECK URGENCY
this is how you tie videos directly to revenue
add urgency to the ads so you can prove that YOUR videos are making them money
how to add urgency:
● "exclusive for people seeing this ad"
● "only available until [specific date]"
● "first 10 people get this bonus"
● tie it to a holiday or event
urgency = immediate action = trackable conversions
this is how you prove your value
organic content doesn't give you this level of control
ads are predictable - you pay x dollars, x people see it, y people convert
STEP 3: ANALYZE THE PAIN ANGLE
can you pull the pain trigger in your ads?
you can't do it with every niche, but when you can, it's powerful
example: hurricane-proof windows in South Florida
● pain: your family could literally die if debris breaks your window during a hurricane
● hook: show a storm, rock comes through window
● urgency: hurricane season is coming
● outcome: life or death decision
other pain-driven niches:
● weight loss (health risks, embarrassment)
● financial services (losing money, bankruptcy)
● home security (break-ins, theft)
● medical (untreated conditions getting worse)
use ChatGPT's deep research feature: "what are the pain points for [specific ICP] in [specific
industry]"
test pain-driven ads vs. aspiration-driven ads
see which converts better
STEP 4: COSMETIC VS LIFE-CHANGING
is this a nice-to-have or a need-to-have?
cosmetic:
● new kitchen countertops
● landscaping upgrades
● aesthetic improvements
life-changing:
● hurricane-proof windows
● medical treatments
● emergency home repairs
● pest control
if it's life-changing, lean HARD into consequences of inaction
if it's cosmetic, lean into aspiration and status
STEP 5: AUDIT THE HOOK AND VISUAL
decision A: is the hook scroll-stopping?
you have no idea how many AI filmmakers fuck this up
the first 3 seconds is EVERYTHING
everyone and their mother is running ads nowadays
people are dopamine monkeys
you need scroll-stopping hooks or the ad dies
decision B: does it speak directly to the client's ICP?
this is super important
when you're running ads, the first thing the viewer sees is the visual
then they hear what you say
you see first, then you hear - that's how we interpret things
example:
● selling pool decks for mansions
● option 1: owner standing in front of massive beautiful pool deck at a mansion
● option 2: owner standing in front of an RV home
which one speaks to wealthy homeowners?
obviously option 1
decision C: do visuals match ICP preferences?
● high-end service: polished, professional imagery
● blue-collar service: authentic, relatable
● emergency service: urgency-driven visuals
attention to detail is everything
your ads need to LOOK like they're for the right audience
STEP 6: IMPLEMENT ALL FOUR ELEMENTS
make sure your ad has:
1. valuable offer (not homework)
2. clear urgency mechanism
3. pain or aspiration angle
4. scroll-stopping hook + ICP-matched visuals
STEP 7: TEST AND MEASURE RESULTS
we test ads really quickly
we know when one's not working
when one's not working, we spin off a completely different ad with different messaging, maybe
different visuals
our testing system:
● collect massive amounts of B-roll from clients (or have them record on iPhone)
Or generate them with ai, sora 2 works really good for broll
● use 11 Labs to clone their voice or create AI voiceover
● test different scripts with the same B-roll
● test different visuals with the same script
● find winners, scale them
we're basically just testing scripts and offers using AI voiceovers
it's the easiest way to test ads
you need to leverage AI or AI will replace you
KEY METRICS TO WATCH
for paid ads, there's mainly one metric we watch:
unique outbound clicks (as % of impressions):
● below 1% = kill campaign
● above 1% = it's good
● above 2% = it's really good
● above 3% = fucking great, scale it
THE RAPID TESTING FRAMEWORK

## THE AI CONTENT AGENCY BLUEPRINT

*Source: THE AI CONTENT AGENCY BLUEPRINT (1).pdf*

THE AI CONTENT AGENCY BLUEPRINT
Join my telegram for more sauce like this:
t.me/mikoslab
How to Print Money Selling AI Videos in 2025
every business knows they need videos for ads and social media
but production companies charge $10k+ per video
most can't afford it, so they're sitting there watching competitors win with content while they do
nothing
you can just run an AI content agency to solve this problem
here's the complete blueprint:
POSITIONING YOUR AI VIDEO SERVICE
you need to position AI videos as premium solutions, not budget alternatives
most people fuck this up by competing on price
"hey we're cheaper than a production company"
that's vendor thinking
here's how you actually position this:
tie it to their pain point
your client isn't looking for "a video"
they're looking to solve a problem:
● their ads aren't converting
● their competitors are outpacing them with content
● they're spending $10k per video and can't test enough angles
● they need 50 pieces of content per month but can't afford a full production team
that's what you sell against
speed + volume = competitive advantage
traditional filmmaking takes weeks and costs thousands per video
with AI you can deliver:
● multiple variations in days, not weeks
● 10-20 different hooks tested instead of 1
● faster iteration cycles so they can adapt to what's working
● the ability to test new campaigns without blowing their entire budget on one shoot
don't just pitch "it's cheaper"
pitch "you can now outpace every competitor because you have 10x the creative firepower"
the gain they actually get:
while their competitors are waiting 3 weeks for one video from a production company, your client
is:
● testing 15 different angles
● finding what converts
● scaling the winners
● already profitable before their competitor's single video even launches
that's the real value
speed isn't just about saving time
it's about competitive advantage in a market where whoever tests fastest wins
position yourself as the person who gives them that advantage
not the cheap alternative, but the premium solution that actually makes them money faster
THE AI PRODUCTION WORKFLOW
this is where most people fuck up
they think AI just magically makes good videos
it doesn't
you need a system
MAKING GREAT AI IMAGES
before you touch VEO, you need reference-quality images
here's the workflow I use 90% of the time:
STEP 1: Find Your Reference
● go to Shotdeck (it's a database of movie frames)
● search for the vibe you want (moody boardroom, luxury car, whatever)
● download 3-5 frames that match your vision
STEP 2: Reverse Engineer the Prompt
● upload reference to gemini
● ask: "analyze this image's composition, lighting, camera angle, and color grading"
● copy that description word for word
STEP 3A: For Objects/Environments (no people)
● take that description to Midjourney
● add "--ar 16:9 --style raw" at the end
● generate 4 variations
● pick the best one
STEP 3B: For Character-Driven Scenes this is where it gets good
● go to Freepik
● upload your Shotdeck reference as a style guide
● use their "nano banana" feature to swap elements while keeping composition
● export as high-res
● run it through Seedream 4 at 4K for extra polish
why this workflow destroys Midjourney for characters:
● you get actual control over composition
● character consistency is 10x better
● no fighting with AI interpretation
● colors stay locked in
STEP 4: Final Polish
● check if the image actually tells a story
● make sure the framing matches your shot type
● verify it's 16:9 (VEO requirement)
● save everything organized by project in folders
pro tip: build a library of your best generations so you can reuse angles, lighting setups, and
compositions across different clients
VEO 3.1 PROMPTING FRAMEWORK
VEO transforms text into video with audio
the gap between mediocre and exceptional output comes down to two things: how you structure
your prompts and which workflow you choose
image-to-video dominates our workflow (90% of projects)
text-to-video is reserved for specific scenarios where image generation isn't practical
AUDIO ENGINEERING
VEO generates audio automatically when you prompt for it
think in four layers: dialogue, ambient noise, sound effects, and music
dialogue approaches:
● explicit: "A guy says: My name is Ben"
● implicit: "A guy tells us his name"
keep dialogue under 8 seconds of actual speech
if you find the character is saying your thing too slow, you can actually just add more words in
there
remember veo is working backwards from filling an 8 second frame
adding more words will increase delivery speed (you can always cut it later)
CAMERA CONTROL
VEO understands camera movements exceptionally well
simple prompts work ("zoom in", "pan left"), but professional results require proper terminology
professional camera movements:
● Pan (horizontal rotation)
● Tilt (vertical rotation)
● Push In
● Pull Out
● Zoom
● Dolly Zoom
● Roll
● Tracking
● Arc
● Boom (vertical movement)
● Handheld
● Bird's Eye
for selfie-style POV shots: "A selfie video of [subject], holds the camera at arm's length, arm
clearly visible in frame"
pro tip: maintain a custom GPT trained specifically for camera movements. giving claude or
chatgpt a lot of system knowledge about filmmaking will actually go really far when making shot
lists, storyboards, and your actual video
TEXT-TO-VIDEO (WHEN WE USE IT)
rarely used, but valuable for quick tests, establishing shots, or scenes without specific character
requirements
keep prompts straightforward
JSON formatting only for genuinely complex shots - otherwise, it's overkill
IMAGE-TO-VIDEO (PRIMARY WORKFLOW)
this is how we work most of the time
two distinct approaches depending on what you're creating
WORKFLOW 1: Objects & Environments
best for products, landscapes, architecture, abstract scenes - anything without human subjects
1. find reference on Shotdeck
2. reverse engineer prompt with Gemini
3. generate image in Midjourney (16:9, raw style)
4. feed to VEO 3 as image-to-video
WORKFLOW 2: Character-Driven Scenes
for anything involving people, this produces superior results
1. find reference on Shotdeck
2. import to Freepik as reference
3. replace elements with "nano banana" while maintaining composition and color
4. refine in Seedream 4 at 4K
5. generate video in VEO 3.1
the second workflow's advantage: character consistency and control over composition without
fighting Midjourney's interpretation
TECHNICAL SPECS & UPSCALING
VEO 3 outputs at 720p, 16:9 aspect ratio by default
for upscaling: topaz remains the best tool, but use at caution as it can make it look cartoonish
at times
boost frame rate to 60fps for smoother motion when needed, particularly for action sequences
process order matters: generate at native resolution first, upscale as the final step
PROMPT STRUCTURE TEMPLATE
[Technical specs] [Subject] [Action] in [Context]. [Camera movement] captures
[Composition]. [Lighting/ambiance]. [Audio elements]. (no subtitles!)
Spoken lines:
Character: dialogue
You can also use the snubroot veo 3 meta github and upload this to a claude project and let it
create prompts for you:
https://github.com/snubroot/Veo-3-Meta-Framework
More sauce:
TOOL STACK SUMMARY
● Shotdeck (references)
● Gemini (prompt engineering)
● Midjourney (objects/scenery images)
● Freepik + Seedream 4 (character images)
● VEO 3/3.1 (video generation)
● Astra (upscaling)
● Custom Camera GPT (professional movements)
the separation between amateur and professional output is knowing which workflow fits your
specific shot and executing it cleanly
image-to-video dominates because it gives you control over the starting frame
text-to-video remains useful for quick iterations
use proper camera terminology
trust the physics engine
keep dialogue concise
upscale strategically
THE QUALITY SECRET (8K SHOT
PROMPTING)
here's something most people don't know:
VEO 3 FOLLOWS CAMERA PROMPTING STRUCTURES EXTREMELY WELL the
github framework i mentioned doesn't include 4k or 8k shot prompting by default so
before you send your final prompt to veo 3, you need to add this:
in your claude chat, tell it:
"make sure this scene is shot on a [SPECIFIC 8K CAMERA MODEL] with ultra-detailed 8K
resolution"
have it specify the actual camera - like "RED Komodo 6K" or "ARRI Alexa LF" or "Sony
Venice 8K"
this COMPLETELY changes the output quality
veo 3 understands cinematography language and will adjust the rendering to match that
camera's characteristics
8K SHOT PROMPTING CHANGED EVERYTHING FOR ME
suddenly my videos went from "pretty good" to "wait this looks like a real commercial"
Sora 2 is also another tool that works great and ill be going over this in future guides and my
telegram about the insane use cases for this.
BECOMING A REVENUE PARTNER (NOT JUST A
VENDOR)
look, you can post the AI videos you make organically and try to get clients like
that but it's not a guaranteed result
the algorithm is unpredictable, views are unreliable, and you can't prove ROI
if you want your clients to actually have results from your AI videos, you need to actually run ads
for them and combine this service with marketing
here's the truth: one-and-done AI videos are a commodity
you will be a freelancer forever if that's your game
constantly searching for the next client to pay your rent
your goal is to sign retainers on these AI videos, make sure they're getting results, and ensure
the videos are performing well
that's how you actually get paid long term
that's how you stop living project-to-project
here's the brutal truth: most AI filmmakers think just making pretty videos is enough
it's not
your only play is to climb the value ladder
stop being the guy who just delivers videos
become the guy who makes your clients money
THE 4-PART MARKETING FUNNEL
most AI filmmakers only focus on generating attention
that's the lowest value part of the funnel
here's what you need to understand:
1. generating attention (what most videographers do)
2. generating leads (where you should start)
3. generating appointments (where you make real money)
4. closing sales (where you become irreplaceable)
the closer you get to the money, the more money you make
sales agencies make millions per month because they operate the whole funnel
you need to get closer to the sale
THE CLIENT CAMPAIGN AUDIT PROCESS
here's our step-by-step framework for making ads that actually
convert: STEP 1: ANALYZE THE CURRENT OFFER
is it homework or is it value?
homework = bad:
● "call me for a quote"
● "click to learn more"
● no real incentive
value = good:
● "10% off if you book this week"
● "free [something] with your order"
● "limited time holiday offer"
think about it like this: should a complete stranger go out of their way to click the link down
below, fill out their information, and go through the process?
is it worth their time?
if the answer is no, that's homework
and if it's homework, the ads aren't going to perform well
examples of good offers:
● holiday specials (Labor Day, July 4th, etc)
● "Independence Day offer - limited time only get 10% off your new kitchen"
● "exclusive for people watching this ad"
● free upgrades or add-ons
STEP 2: CHECK URGENCY
this is how you tie videos directly to revenue
add urgency to the ads so you can prove that YOUR videos are making them money
how to add urgency:
● "exclusive for people seeing this ad"
● "only available until [specific date]"
● "first 10 people get this bonus"
● tie it to a holiday or event
urgency = immediate action = trackable conversions
this is how you prove your value
organic content doesn't give you this level of control
ads are predictable - you pay x dollars, x people see it, y people convert
STEP 3: ANALYZE THE PAIN ANGLE
can you pull the pain trigger in your ads?
you can't do it with every niche, but when you can, it's powerful
example: hurricane-proof windows in South Florida
● pain: your family could literally die if debris breaks your window during a hurricane
● hook: show a storm, rock comes through window
● urgency: hurricane season is coming
● outcome: life or death decision
other pain-driven niches:
● weight loss (health risks, embarrassment)
● financial services (losing money, bankruptcy)
● home security (break-ins, theft)
● medical (untreated conditions getting worse)
use ChatGPT's deep research feature: "what are the pain points for [specific ICP] in [specific
industry]"
test pain-driven ads vs. aspiration-driven ads
see which converts better
STEP 4: COSMETIC VS LIFE-CHANGING
is this a nice-to-have or a need-to-have?
cosmetic:
● new kitchen countertops
● landscaping upgrades
● aesthetic improvements
life-changing:
● hurricane-proof windows
● medical treatments
● emergency home repairs
● pest control
if it's life-changing, lean HARD into consequences of inaction
if it's cosmetic, lean into aspiration and status
STEP 5: AUDIT THE HOOK AND VISUAL
decision A: is the hook scroll-stopping?
you have no idea how many AI filmmakers fuck this up
the first 3 seconds is EVERYTHING
everyone and their mother is running ads nowadays
people are dopamine monkeys
you need scroll-stopping hooks or the ad dies
decision B: does it speak directly to the client's ICP?
this is super important
when you're running ads, the first thing the viewer sees is the visual
then they hear what you say
you see first, then you hear - that's how we interpret things
example:
● selling pool decks for mansions
● option 1: owner standing in front of massive beautiful pool deck at a mansion
● option 2: owner standing in front of an RV home
which one speaks to wealthy homeowners?
obviously option 1
decision C: do visuals match ICP preferences?
● high-end service: polished, professional imagery
● blue-collar service: authentic, relatable
● emergency service: urgency-driven visuals
attention to detail is everything
your ads need to LOOK like they're for the right audience
STEP 6: IMPLEMENT ALL FOUR ELEMENTS
make sure your ad has:
1. valuable offer (not homework)
2. clear urgency mechanism
3. pain or aspiration angle
4. scroll-stopping hook + ICP-matched visuals
STEP 7: TEST AND MEASURE RESULTS
we test ads really quickly
we know when one's not working
when one's not working, we spin off a completely different ad with different messaging, maybe
different visuals
our testing system:
● collect massive amounts of B-roll from clients (or have them record on iPhone)
Or generate them with ai, sora 2 works really good for broll
● use 11 Labs to clone their voice or create AI voiceover
● test different scripts with the same B-roll
● test different visuals with the same script
● find winners, scale them
we're basically just testing scripts and offers using AI voiceovers
it's the easiest way to test ads
you need to leverage AI or AI will replace you
KEY METRICS TO WATCH
